Yes. It's not that people hadn't previously thought that evolution could illuminate psychology. Darwin thought that, right? I mean, and Darwin thought that there had been various attempts to take on psychology with an evolutionary lens. I mean, William James thought that.
Jean Bowlby certainly thought that. I mean, Jean Bowlby was, I consider him an evolutionary psychologist with attachment theory. He started with like primate behavior and he thought that clearly there were mechanisms that kept an infant monkey staying within range of his mother and going back to her when frightened and using her as a base of exploration. He thought that was an evolved mechanism.
But there wasn't a language. And even when you look at the early mythologists who I do think of as cognitive psychologists, they were still doing things before cognitive psychology was really much of a thing. And so they're grasping towards explanations.
And that's why they talk about things like fixed action patterns and so forth, because I think it's because they lack a more fuller -throated language of information processing. Yeah, yeah And so, yes, this cognitive revolution was happening around the same time. It was full of revolutions.
